core_payload.content:
# Background Agent Runtime Mutation Gates

## 1. Boundary
Background Agent Runtime Mutation Gates owns the permission layer that decides whether background work may safely cross from observation into mutation. It groups readiness, workspace profiling, deep intake risk handling, branch discipline, validation authority, and review pressure so runtime power never outruns runtime law.

## 2. What Belongs Here
This sub-hub carries the pre-mutation and review-side atoms: `agent-activation-readiness`, `workspace-recon`, `deep-intake-investigation`, `validation-gatekeeper-agent`, `branch-steward-agent`, and `review-agent`. Together they answer whether the runtime may act, what environment it is acting in, what risk profile the intake carries, and which governance gate can stop or redirect the work.

## 3. What Does Not Belong Here
This sub-hub does not own daemon uptime, assignment continuity, or job scheduling. Those live in `daemon-lifecycle`, `control-plane`, and `work-orchestration`. Mutation gates define permission and review discipline, not runtime survival and not queue throughput.

neuro_concentrate.summary: Background Agent Runtime Mutation Gates is the permission layer of the runtime. It groups activation readiness, workspace recon, deep intake investigation, branch stewardship, validation gatekeeping, and review so background mutation stays governed before work crosses from observation into change. This boundary matters because runtime power should never outrun runtime law: permission, environment classification, risk posture, and review pressure need one explicit surface that can halt or redirect work before mutation begins. It gives the background runtime a clear governance seam without confusing policy authority with daemon uptime or queue throughput.

epistemic_ledger.rationale: Minted as part of background-agent-runtime hub decomposition to reduce parent link density from 44 outbound to ≤15.
Expanded the mutation-gates summary to satisfy validator length requirements and normalized the semantic hash to an eight-token canonical form while preserving the existing permission boundary.
